wafv2NoRules.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have zero rules configured. An empty WebACL applies only its DefaultAction to every request — if DefaultAction=Allow, the WAF is a pass-through that provides no protection. Empty WebACLs still cost the base $5/month.
wafv2NoRules.shortDesc: Add rules (managed or custom) to every WebACL

wafv2NoManagedRuleGroups.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) contain no managed rule groups (AWS or Marketplace). Managed rule groups protect against OWASP Top 10 risks, known bad inputs, bots, and reputation-based threats. Hand-rolled rules alone rarely provide equivalent coverage. Advisory: teams that front their traffic with a third-party WAF (Cloudflare, Akamai, Imperva, F5) may run AWS WAF only as a defence-in-depth layer with custom rules — suppress this finding if that is your architecture.
wafv2NoManagedRuleGroups.shortDesc: Attach at least one AWS-managed rule group

wafv2NoRateBasedRules.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have no rate-based rules. Rate-based rules count requests per 5-minute window per IP (or forwarded IP / custom aggregate key) and are the primary defence against brute-force login, credential stuffing, scraping, and volumetric application-layer DDoS. Advisory: teams that rely on a third-party WAF, CloudFront/Shield Advanced rate limiting, or upstream API-Gateway usage plans for rate control may suppress this finding.
wafv2NoRateBasedRules.shortDesc: Add a rate-based rule sized for your traffic profile

wafv2RulesInCountMode.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have one or more CUSTOM (non-managed) rules with Action=Count. COUNT mode is intended for tuning — matched requests are logged and metered but NOT blocked. Custom rules left in COUNT after tuning provide zero protection despite appearing configured. Advisory: managed rule groups with OverrideAction=Count are handled by wafv2NoManagedRuleGroups and are not double-flagged here. Third-party WAF users who use AWS WAF only for observability may deliberately keep custom rules in Count — suppress if that is your architecture.
wafv2RulesInCountMode.shortDesc: Move tuned custom rules from COUNT to BLOCK / CAPTCHA / CHALLENGE

wafv2DefaultActionAllow.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have DefaultAction=Allow AND no rule that Blocks/Captchas/Challenges anything. This is a pass-through WAF: every request that doesn't explicitly match an Allow rule still hits the origin unfiltered. Either flip to DefaultAction=Block with explicit Allow rules, or add real Block/Captcha/Challenge rules.
wafv2DefaultActionAllow.shortDesc: Do not run a WebACL with DefaultAction=Allow and no blocking rules

wafv2LoggingNotConfigured.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have no logging configuration (GetLoggingConfiguration returns WAFNonexistentItemException). Without logging, blocked/allowed requests cannot be audited, false positives cannot be triaged, and incident response has no forensic trail.
wafv2LoggingNotConfigured.shortDesc: Enable logging to CloudWatch Logs / S3 / Firehose

wafv2LoggingFilterAllDrop.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have logging configured but the LoggingFilter has DefaultBehavior=DROP with no explicit KEEP filters (or the filters KEEP nothing). Effectively no traffic is logged — the illusion of logging without any actual records.
wafv2LoggingFilterAllDrop.shortDesc: Fix LoggingFilter — either remove it or KEEP the traffic you care about

wafv2CloudWatchMetricsDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have CloudWatchMetricsEnabled=false at the WebACL level. Without CloudWatch metrics, you cannot alarm on blocked-request spikes, cannot dashboard block/allow ratios, and cannot detect attacks in near-real-time.

wafv2SampledRequestsDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have SampledRequestsEnabled=false. Without sampled requests, the console cannot show which requests matched a rule — making false-positive triage and rule tuning nearly impossible.

wafv2RuleVisibilityDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) contain individual rule(s) with CloudWatchMetricsEnabled=false or SampledRequestsEnabled=false in their per-rule VisibilityConfig. Without per-rule metrics you cannot compare rule effectiveness, and without per-rule sampled requests you cannot see which requests any given rule matched.
wafv2RuleVisibilityDisabled.shortDesc: Enable CloudWatchMetricsEnabled and SampledRequestsEnabled on every rule

wafv2NotAssociated.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) are not associated with any resource (ALB / API Gateway / AppSync / Cognito user pool / App Runner for REGIONAL; CloudFront distribution for CLOUDFRONT). Unassociated WebACLs still incur the ~$5/month base charge and the per-rule charge without providing any protection. Note: for CLOUDFRONT-scoped WebACLs the scanner uses cloudfront:ListDistributionsByWebACLId; if that permission is missing, the check is downgraded to INFO instead of failing.
wafv2NotAssociated.shortDesc: Associate the WebACL with a resource, or delete it

wafv2ResourcesWithoutTags.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have no tags applied. Tags enable cost allocation across environments, tag-based IAM (aws:ResourceTag/... conditions), and lifecycle governance.

wafv2NoIpReputationList.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have managed rule groups configured but do NOT include AWSManagedRulesAmazonIpReputationList. This managed group blocks requests from IPs recently observed in AWS-tracked malicious activity — the lowest-cost, highest-signal reputation defense AWS offers. Only flagged when the ACL already uses at least one managed group; empty ACLs are covered by wafv2NoRules / wafv2NoManagedRuleGroups.

wafv2NoSqlInjectionProtection.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have no SQL-injection protection: neither AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet nor any custom rule with SqliMatchStatement (statements nested inside And/Or/Not/RateBased are checked recursively).
wafv2NoSqlInjectionProtection.shortDesc: Add SQLi protection (managed SQLi ruleset or custom SqliMatchStatement)

wafv2NoXssProtection.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have no XSS protection: no AWS-managed group that covers XSS (CommonRuleSet includes XSS body rules) AND no custom rule with XssMatchStatement. Recursive statement walk applied.
wafv2NoXssProtection.shortDesc: Add XSS protection (Common Rule Set or custom XssMatchStatement)

wafv2HighCapacityUsage.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) consume >80% of the WCU (Web ACL Capacity Unit) limit for their scope. REGIONAL WebACLs are capped at 5000 WCUs; CLOUDFRONT WebACLs at 1500 WCUs. Approaching the limit blocks adding new rules and can cause updates to fail.
wafv2HighCapacityUsage.shortDesc: Reduce WebACL capacity — request a limit increase or split rules

wafv2ManagedRuleGroupAllCountOverride.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) contain managed rule group(s) whose entire rule set is neutered: every rule is either in ExcludedRules or has a RuleActionOverride to Count. The managed group is effectively disabled while looking configured.
wafv2ManagedRuleGroupAllCountOverride.shortDesc: Remove blanket Count/Excluded overrides from managed rule groups

wafv2NoCoreRuleSet.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) attach at least one managed rule group but do NOT include AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet. The Core Rule Set is the foundational (free) AWS managed group covering OWASP baseline patterns (Body, URI, Query-string, XSS surface) and should be present on every internet-facing WebACL.

wafv2AllowRuleBeforeManagedRules.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have a custom Allow-action rule at a lower Priority (runs earlier) than a managed rule group. Traffic matched by the Allow rule bypasses every managed rule downstream — a common misconfiguration when a hasty IP allow-list is added above the managed baseline.
wafv2AllowRuleBeforeManagedRules.shortDesc: Move Allow rules AFTER managed rule groups (higher Priority number)

wafv2ManagedRuleGroupVersionPinned.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) pin a specific version of a managed rule group (VersionName set) instead of using the default auto-updating alias. Pinned versions have an ExpiryTimestamp — a pinned-and-expired managed group stops receiving updates and eventually starts throwing errors when queried. Escalates to FAIL when the pinned version expires within 30 days.
wafv2ManagedRuleGroupVersionPinned.shortDesc: Unpin managed rule group versions or rotate before expiry

wafv2NoBotControl.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) protect internet-facing resources but do not attach AWSManagedRulesBotControlRuleSet. Bot Control is a paid add-on and not appropriate everywhere, but resource-facing ACLs with credential-stuffing / scraping exposure should consider it. Informational only.

wafv2RateBasedRuleThresholdTooHigh.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have rate-based rule(s) with Limit > 10000 requests per 5 minutes. At that threshold most credential-stuffing / abuse traffic never trips the rule. Common baseline is 2000 req/5min per IP.
wafv2RateBasedRuleThresholdTooHigh.shortDesc: Tune rate-based rule Limit downward (2000-5000 per 5min is typical)

wafv2LoggingFilterDropsBlocked.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) have a LoggingFilter that drops BLOCK-action requests. Attack traffic that the WAF actually stops is exactly the evidence you need for forensics — dropping it means the WAF looks clean while incidents go unrecorded.

wafv2IpSetOverlyPermissive.^description: {$COUNT} WAFv2 WebACL(s) reference an IP set (in an Allow-action rule) whose Addresses contain overly-broad CIDRs (/8 or shorter for IPv4). A broad allow-list defeats the WAF for everything inside the CIDR range.
wafv2IpSetOverlyPermissive.shortDesc: Restrict allow-list IP sets to specific CIDRs
